title: Climate Change and Environment
gist: This is a PIIE topic-landing page aggregating links and headlines from its climate/environment coverage (research briefs, testimony, and 'Policy for the Planet' podcast episodes), not a single substantive essay. The only real content is a set of headline claims and topic teasers spanning El Nino's economic cost, climate policy affordability, data center energy demand, critical minerals geopolitics, and local climate policy.

themes.theme: Economic costs of climate and energy transition

insights.claim: PIIE research by Cullen S. Hendrix estimates that an El Nino event could drag down the global economy by a range as wide as roughly $700 billion to $3 trillion, with the outcome framed as a matter of policy choice rather than fixed physical inevitability.
insights.speaker: Peterson Institute (PIIE)
insights.so_what: A 4x-plus spread in projected El Nino damages implies large tail risk to global growth forecasts that is highly sensitive to policy response, worth tracking as a macro variable in commodity and EM-growth models.
insights.quote: El Niño could drag down the global economy by almost $700 billion or $3 trillion—and the choice is ours
insights.locator: El Niño could drag down the global economy

insights.claim: PIIE is running a live debate (featuring Monica de Bolle and Douglas Giuffre of S&P Global Energy) on whether data centers are driving up utility costs for consumers.
insights.speaker: Peterson Institute (PIIE)
insights.so_what: Signals that AI/data-center electricity demand and its pass-through to retail utility rates is an emerging policy and investor concern worth monitoring alongside utility-sector earnings and regulatory dockets.
insights.quote: Are data centers driving up utility costs?

insights.claim: Kimberly Clausing of PIIE frames delayed climate action explicitly as an affordability problem rather than purely an environmental one, in testimony titled 'Climate Inaction is an Affordability Problem.'
insights.speaker: Peterson Institute (PIIE)
insights.so_what: Reframing climate delay as a cost-of-living issue is a messaging and policy angle that could shift how climate legislation is sold politically, relevant for anticipating US climate/energy policy direction.
insights.quote: Climate Inaction is an Affordability Problem

insights.claim: PIIE's Cullen S. Hendrix has covered the geopolitics behind critical minerals as a distinct topic within its climate/energy coverage, tying mineral supply chains to broader climate-transition strategy.
insights.speaker: Peterson Institute (PIIE)
insights.so_what: Confirms critical minerals supply chains remain a live geopolitical flashpoint tied to the energy transition, relevant for mining/materials exposure and China-related supply chain risk.

insights.claim: PIIE examined Germany as a case study on whether local (rather than national) policies can relieve public fatigue with climate activism, featuring Clemens Helbach of the German Embassy in Washington.
insights.speaker: Peterson Institute (PIIE)
insights.so_what: Suggests localization of climate policy is being explored as a response to activism fatigue in developed economies, a political-durability factor for long-horizon climate policy bets.
insights.quote: Can local policies relieve climate activism fatigue? Germany’s case
